coredata.dc:title: Theoretical insights into tunable electronic properties of graphene quantum dots through ZnO doping
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ajeel, Fouad N.
Mohsin, Kareem H.
Shakier, Hussein G.
Khamees, Salam K.
Mutier, Mohammed N.
coredata.dc:description: Quantum dots are interesting systems because they can be tuned electronically. We used graphene quantum dots (GQDs) known as the formula C24H12 as a model for studying the effect of zinc oxide (ZnO) dimers on the structural and electronic properties of GQDs by density functional theory (DFT) investigation. By modifying a GQD geometrical pattern with ZnO dimers, three different forms of ZnO dimers are used to create the zinc and oxygen atoms in the ortho, meta, and para positions. Based on our findings, it has been found that the bandgap values of the GQD structure are significantly influenced by the presence and form of ZnO dimers. the electronic characteristics of GQD are affected not only by the number of GQD dimers present but also by the arrangement of ZnO dimers within the GQD. By suitably doping ZnO dimers, we can tune the bandgap of GQDs. These findings provide insight into how chemical doping affects the electronic characteristics of GQDs and motivate the design of nanodevices with improved electronic performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
ZnO dimers
Bandgap
Chemical doping
DFT

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Pure graphene quantum dot structure, and their (b) their density of state, (c) structured geometries and their bond lengths, (d) molecular electrostatic potential graphic considered in the gas phase.
The structures of graphene quantum dot with ZnO dimers forming (a) ZnO-1, (b) ZnO-2, (c) ZnO −3, and (d) ZnO-4 structures with their density of state.
The structured geometries and the bond lengths in the angstrom unit of the graphene quantum dot with ZnO dimers forming (a) ZnO-1, (b) ZnO-2, (c) ZnO-3, and (d) ZnO-4 structures.
The energy gap of (1) pristine GQD, and GQD with (2) ZnO-1, (3) ZnO-2, (4) ZnO-3, and (5) ZnO-4 structures, respectively.

The molecular electrostatic potential graphic calculated in the gas phase of graphene quantum dot with ZnO dimers forming (a) ZnO-1, (b) ZnO-2, (c) ZnO-3, and (d) ZnO-4 structures. A spectrum of different colors, with red having the lowest electrostatic potential energy values and blue having the greatest.

The global chemical indexes: the ionization potential (IP), electron affinity (EA), chemical potential (μ), chemical hardness (η), and electrophilicity (ω) calculated for graphene quantum dot with and without ZnO dimers.

head.ce:title: Theoretical insights into tunable electronic properties of graphene quantum dots through ZnO doping

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, College of Science, University of Sfax, Tunisia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Physics, College of Science, University of Sfax, Tunisia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, College of Science, University of Sumer, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Physics, College of Science, University of Sumer, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Directorate of Education, Thi-qar, Ministry of Education, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDirectorate of Education, Thi-qar, Ministry of Education, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum dots are interesting systems because they can be tuned electronically. We used graphene quantum dots (GQDs) known as the formula C24H12 as a model for studying the effect of zinc oxide (ZnO) dimers on the structural and electronic properties of GQDs by density functional theory (DFT) investigation. By modifying a GQD geometrical pattern with ZnO dimers, three different forms of ZnO dimers are used to create the zinc and oxygen atoms in the ortho, meta, and para positions. Based on our findings, it has been found that the bandgap values of the GQD structure are significantly influenced by the presence and form of ZnO dimers. the electronic characteristics of GQD are affected not only by the number of GQD dimers present but also by the arrangement of ZnO dimers within the GQD. By suitably doping ZnO dimers, we can tune the bandgap of GQDs. These findings provide insight into how chemical doping affects the electronic characteristics of GQDs and motivate the design of nanodevices with improved electronic performance.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional graphene has fascinating electronic and optical characteristics, making it one of the most studied nanoscale materials [1,2]. There is great technological potential in graphene sheets due to its ability to be doped, functionalized, and self-organized [3–9]. Due to the fact that graphene is a zero overlap semi-metal (both electrons and holes are carry a charge), it has garnered a lot of research attention in a variety of applications in which high conductivity has been a barrier [10,11]. It features a special electrical band structure highlighted by the massless Dirac spectrum, leading to unusual physical characteristics [12–15]. On the other hand, the challenge of the zero-band gap might be treated by cutting graphene into nanoribbons or quantum dots (QDs), which decreases the lateral dimensions of the material [11,16-18]. Graphene quantum dots, also known as GQDs, are nanoparticles constructed of graphene that have a size of less than 100 nanometers [19,20]. Because of their remarkable properties, such as low toxicity, increased photoluminescence, chemical stability, and an effective quantum confinement effect, GQDs are considered as a one-of-a-kind material that may be used in biological, optoelectronic, energy, and environmental applications. This is due to the fact that their photoluminescence can be maintained for a longer period of time [21–23].

It is essential to be able to modify the properties of GQDs in order to make use of them in a wide variety of applications [24–27]. In 2012, Zhao and coworkers explored doping as a method of modifying the properties of GQDs [28], specifically focusing on the use of nitrogen as the dopant. Additionally, GQDs have higher solubility than CNTs. Unlike CNTs, which are constrained by their one-dimensional features, the edge effect of GQDs may be modified by functional groups, potentially increasing their performance [24]. Graphene lattices are present inside of GQDs of any size. It doesn't matter how small they are. GQDs have improved optoelectronic characteristics compared to those of regular QDs as a result of quantum confinement and the edge effect [29]. GQDs have numerous distinctive characteristics with graphene. Altering the size, shape, and edge morphology of GQDs, as well as the number of layers and the external electric and magnetic fields that are applied, are all effective ways of changing the bandgap of these materials [30–36]. GQDs, in addition to having a bandgap that can be tuned, also include hidden energy levels that can be implemented in quantum computers as qubits [37,38]. Due to the presence of functional groups at the edges, the GQDs are chemically and physically stable, have a high surface-to-mass ratio, and are efficiently diffused in water [24,39].

In this study, we studied the structural, stability, and electronic characteristics of graphene quantum dots that were both pristine and ZnO-codoped graphene quantum dots in various locations. As a result, dimers of type ortho, meta, and para are found at the same Zn/O atom concentration. For usage in nanoelectronics, biosensors, and nanomedicine applications, these three ZnO dimers are employed to improve the electronic properties of graphene quantum dots.

Here, we are trying to attain is to study the electronic properties of graphene quantum dot (C24H12), which has a 7-benzene ring in its structure as seen in Fig. 1 [40,41]. So, we need to solve the many-body Schrödinger equation to find the Hamiltonian of the system. However, solving the Schrödinger equation for several interacting particles be used approximations such as density functional theory (DFT) [42]. In the fields of condensed-matter physics, computational physics, and computational chemistry, DFT is one of the techniques that is considered to be the most widely used and flexible. The DFT is a computational quantum mechanical modeling tool that is used to investigate the electronic structure (mostly the ground state) of many-body systems [43]. These many-body systems include atoms, molecules, and the condensed phases. Functionals, also known as functions of another function, may be used in agreement with this theory in order determine the features of a many-electron system. In the framework of DFT, they are functionals of the spatially dependent electron density [43].

In the Born-Oppenheimer approximation, the nuclei of the molecules being treated are assumed to be fixed, leading to a constant external potential V in which the molecules' electrons move. When the many-electron time-independent Schrödinger equation is satisfied, the corresponding electronic ground state may be characterized by the wavefunction
Ψ
(
r 1
,
… ,
r N
)
[42]:
(1)
H ^
Ψ
=
[
T ^
+
V ^
+
U ^
]
Ψ
=
[
∑
i = 1
N
(
−
ℏ 2
2
m i
∇ i 2
)
+
∑
i = 1
N
V
(
r i
)
+
∑
i < j
N
U
(
r i
,
r j
)
]
Ψ
= E
Ψ
where, for the N -electron system,
H ^
is the Hamiltonian,
T ^
is the kinetic energy,
V ^
is the potential energy from the external field due to positively charged nuclei, and
U ^
is the electron-electron interaction energy. Due to the presence of the interaction term
U ^
, this complex many-particle equation cannot be decomposed into simpler single-particle equations.

The many-body Schrodinger equation may be solved using a variety of complex approaches, many of which are based on the expansion of the wavefunction into Slater determinants. Here, DFT provides a strong approach that is considerably more adaptable, since it gives a mechanism to systematically apply the many-body problem, with
U ^
, onto a single-body problem that does not include
U ^
. This makes DFT an appealing method. In DFT, the electron density, denoted by
n ( r )
, is the most important variable; its value is calculated using the following formula for a normalized
Ψ
[44]:
(2)
n
( r )
= N ∫
d 3
r 2
… ∫
d 3
r N
Ψ
*
(
r ,
r 2
, … ,
r N
)
Ψ
(
r ,
r 2
, … ,
r N
)
This relationship may be inverted, such that the ground-state wavefunction
Ψ
(
r 1
,
… ,
r N
)
can be calculated from a known density
n 0
( r )
. In other words,
Ψ
is a unique functional of
n 0
,
Ψ
0
=
Ψ
[
n 0
]
, and so the expected value of an observable
O ^
in the ground state is similarly a function of
n 0
:
(3)
O
[
n 0
]
=

Ψ
[
n 0
]

[

]
〉

In specifically, the energy of the ground state is a functional of
n 0
, which appears to be:
(4)
E 0

[

]
=
〈
Ψ
[

]
|

V ^

U ^
|
Ψ
[

]
〉
where the influence of the external potential
〈
Ψ
[
n 0
]
|
V ^
|
Ψ
[
n 0
]
〉
may be expressed clearly in terms of the ground-state density n :
(5)
V

n 0
]
= ∫ V
( r )
n 0

d 3
r
In most cases, it is possible to provide a representation of the external potential
〈
Ψ
|
V ^
|
Ψ
〉
in terms of the density n
[44]:
(6)
V
[ n ]

Having definite a system, i.e., having definite
V ^
, one need to minimize the functional:
(7)
E
[ n ]
= T
[ n ]
+ U
[ n ]

( r )
n
( r )

r
assuming one has accurate formulations for
T [ n ]
and
U [ n ]
, with regard to
n ( r )
. The ground-state density
n 0
, and all other ground-state observables may be obtained by minimizing the energy functional.

The Lagrangian technique of indeterminate multipliers provides a solution to variational problems including the minimization of the energy functional
E [ n ]
. To begin, an energy functional is taken into account that does not include an electron-electron interaction energy part.
(8)
E s
[ n ]
=
〈
Ψ
s
[ n ]
|
T ^
+
V ^
s
|
Ψ
s
[ n ]
〉
where
T ^
denotes the kinetic-energy operator, and
V ^
s
is an effective potential in which the particles are moving. Based on
E s
, Kohn-Sham equations of this auxiliary noninteracting system can be derived:
(9)
[
−

2
m

V s
( r )
]
φ i
( r )

i
φ i
( r )
which yields the orbitals
φ i
that reproduce the density
n ( r )
of the original many-body system:
(10)
n
( r )
=
∑
i = 1
N
|
φ i
( r )
|
2

The effective single-particle potential may be expressed as the following expression:
(11)
V s
= V
( r )
+ ∫
n
(
r ′
)
|
r −
r ′
|
d 3
r ′
+
V
X C
[
n
( r )
]
where first, second, and third terms denote the external potential, Hartree term describing the electron-electron Coulomb repulsion, and exchange-correlation potential, respectively. Here,
V
X C
contains the interactions of the many-particles. The problem of solving the Kohn-Sham equation has to be done in a self-consistent (i.e., iterative) way. Generally, one starts with an initial guess for
n ( r )
, then estimates the
V s
and solves the Kohn-Sham equations for
φ i
. Following the calculation of this new density, one returns to the beginning. Next, this process is done as many times as necessary until convergence is achieved [42].

The main issue with DFT is its absence of knowledge of the exact functionals for exchange and correlation. However, approximations exist that allow for calculation of physical quantities with a high degree of accuracy. The local-density approximation (LDA) is one of the simplest approximations since the functional only relies on the density at the position where the functional is evaluated [43]:
(12)
E
x c
L D A
[ n ]
= ∫
ε
x c
[ n ]
n
( r )
d 3
r

The LDA presupposes uniform density everywhere. The LDA tends to overestimate the correlation energy and underestimate the exchange energy as a result of this. In general, errors introduced by the exchange and correlation sections tend to cancel each other out. Because the real electron density is not uniform, it is usual practice to compensate for this effect by expanding in terms of the density gradient. This enables for adjustments to be made depending on the variance in density from the coordinate. Generalized gradient approximations (GGA) relate to these types of expansions [43]:
(13)
E
x c
G G A
[ n ]
= ∫
E
x c
[
n , ∇ n
]
n
( r )
d 3
r
through the use of the GGA, great results in terms of molecular geometries and ground-state energies have been realized. With the DFT/GGA method that is included in the Gaussian 09 package, the structure of the GQDs has been optimized on the B3LYP and 6–31 G levels [45]. It is widely known that the 6–31 G core group performs effectively in SCF or engineering enhancements related with closed-shell organic compounds, which is reason it was selected as an option for utilization. Before and after performing the energy calculations, all of the atoms on GQDs with and without ZnO were given the opportunity to relax. Electronic properties of GQDs including DOS resolution, total energies, highest energies of occupied molecular orbitals (
E HOMO
), Fermi level (
E FL
), lowest energies of unoccupied molecular orbitals (
E LUMO
), and bandgap (
E gap
) are studied. Before and after the substitution of C atoms with ZnO impurities at four distinct positions, we investigate and evaluate the electronic characteristics of GQDs.

The adaptability of GQDs as a material has been related to their ability to create and modify their properties via the management of their form and components. The chemical reactivity parameters, which in the context of the DFT include chemical hardness, chemical potential, and the electrophilicity index, are highly useful for analyzing toxicity, evaluating reactivity trend, and exploring excited states [46–48]. The following expressions can be used to define chemical potential and chemical hardness, respectively [48–51]:
(14)
η =
(
I P − E A
)
2
(15)
μ = −
(
I P + E A
)
2
(16)
ω =
μ 2
2 η

It is possible to use Koopman's theorem to get an estimate of the ionization potential (
I P
) and the electron affinity (
E A
) [48,50]:
(17)
I P = −
E
HOMO
(18)
E A = −
E LUMO
where
E
HOMO
and
E LUMO
are the energy levels of the HOMO and LUMO orbitals, respectively.

In addition, we determine the electronic band gap and the Fermi level energy using the following formula [50,52]:
(19)
E g
=
E LUMO
−
E
HOMO
(20)
E FL
=
(
E
HOMO
+
E LUMO
)
2

To investigate the structural stability of the ZnO-codoped GQDs, the formation energies
E F
for all structures were also calculated [53]:
(21)
E f
=
E
ZnO −
codoped
GQD
−
E
GQD
− x
(
E
Zn
+
E
O
)
− 2
E C
here,
E
ZnO −
codoped
GQD
and
E
GQD
are the total energy of the optimized structure of ZnO-codoped GQDs and pure GQD respectively and ‘ x ’ is the number of dopant Al and N atoms.
E
Zn
,
E
O
and
E C
is the total energy of dopant (Zn and O) atoms and carbon atom respectively. The most stability of the doped structures belongs to the most negative
E F
value.

The pure GQDs used in the study were fully tuned at the B3LYP/6–31 G level of theory. To compare the results before and after the addition of ZnO dimers, the geometric and electronic characteristics of GQDs were investigated. Fig. 1 shows the structure of the pure graphene quantum dot (a), their density of states (DOS) of the occupied and unoccupied orbitals of the electron spectrum (b), bond lengths between the carbon atoms in the structure of the molecule (c), and a graphic representation in the gaseous phase to its molecular electrostatic potential (d). The CC bond length was determined to be 1.42 Å Fig. 1(c), which agrees well with published articles [48,54,55]. The results in Fig. 1(b) include the energy gap (
E g
= 4.08 eV) and the HOMO and LUMO in the DOS analysis of pure GQD. It is found that the energy gap value agrees with the references [48,56,57] and indicates the semiconductor behavior of pure GQD with a large bandgap. The imaginary harmonic frequency is calculated and it is zero which demonstrates that the structure has attained a physical stationary state on the potential energy surface.

The purpose of the current study is to determine how different ZnO dimers affect the electronic properties of GQD. We assume four different types of ZnO dimers, as shown in Fig. 2 , including (a) Zn and O atoms doped at ortho position (ZnO-1), (b) Zn or O atom placed next to one another in meta position (ZnO-2), (c) Zn and O are at para position (ZnO-3), and (d) ZnO dimer at ortho position but the distance between two Zn and O atoms is large (ZnO-4). The electronic DOS and the band gap between the valence and conduction bands of nanostructures when ZnO impurities are introduced have been seen in Fig. 2. It was also discovered that the electronic band gap and the DOS diagrams are affected by moving the ZnO impurities. For instance, when the Zn and O atom locations are changed in GQD structures, the gap changes from −26.322% to −67.287%, which is relevant in a variety of scenarios. The energy gap will be reduced, resulting in the transformation of GQD into a semiconductor with a tiny bandgap, by doping GQD structures with ZnO dimers.

The general structural stability of a GQD is also influenced by the bond energies of the CC, C-Zn, CO, and Zn-O bonds. Based on the results in Fig. 3 , we can order the binding energies from most energetic to least energetic as follows: Zn-O > CC > OC > Zn-C. Therefore, the structure that has the most stable will contain more Zn-O bonds than Zn-Zn or OO bonds. Table 1 and Fig. 4 show the DFT results which include the HOMO, Fermi level, LUMO, bandgap, and formation energy of all GQDs before and after the ZnO dimer to demonstrate their structural stability. It is noted that the ZnO-4 structure is the most stable when compared to the values of the other of four ZnO-structures. While the ZnO-2 structures is the most unstable and it is the lowest formation energy. As a result, the most stable structure is the ZnO ortho dimer with a considerable distance, as seen by the formation energy listed in Table 1.

The substitutional doping of Zn and O atoms in GQDs results in different bond lengths and numerous bonds have been found. The average bond length can be ranked from longest to shortest as follows: Zn-C > Zn-O > OC > CC. Due to distortion caused by these different bond lengths, sublattice symmetry in ZnO codoped GQDs would be violated. Due to this, the electronic characteristics of GQDs are significantly altered at high ZnO dimer concentration ratios. The calculated data for the energetic orbitals were also summarized in Table 1. The HOMO and LUMO energies of the pure GQD are −5.487 and −1.403 eV, which is determined by the findings of the frontier molecular orbital investigation. As ZnO dimers are introduced.

In addition, we calculated the overall electron density and constructed a counter map of the molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) in Fig. 5 . The MEP is used to study the molecular characteristics, molecular location, and the capacity to anticipate electrophilic and nucleophilic reactions at the site of doping. The results for the energy surfaces of the MEP of graphene quantum dots with and without ZnO dimers are shown in Fig. 5. Another crucial factor that aids in defining the nature of the interaction is the difference in electronegativity between the two atoms in a chemical bond. The electrostatic potential is represented by the color spectrum.

Utilizing Koopman's theory, the reactivity characteristics listed in Table 2 are assessed. The ionization potential, electron affinity, electronegativity, chemical hardness, chemical softness, and electrophilicity are some of these qualities. They are all very useful for analyzing reactivity patterns, identifying excited states, and determining toxicity [46,47]. The HOMO energy roughly approximates the negative experimental ionization potential, whereas the LUMO energy reflects the negative electron affinity, according to Koopman's theorem [58,59]. The chemical hardness (η) of the system is one of the most valuable markers of a molecule's stability and reactivity. It is anticipated that molecules with the lowest chemical hardness values will act as potential corrosion inhibitors, as demonstrated by the meta position for (ZnO-2)-codoped GQD.

Finally, we must examine the equation
σ ∝ exp (
−
E g
/ 2
k β
T
)
, which shows that a tiny change in the gap can influence the electrical conductivity of the GQD [60,61]. This equation show how ZnO dimers have as important role in the electronic properties of GQDs. Where σ,
k β
, and T are the electrical conductivity, Boltzmann's constant, and 298.14 K of temperature, respectively. The equation demonstrated that higher levels of electric conductivity at a given temperature are caused by lower values of bandgap of the structure. When ZnO dimers are present in pure GQD, this directly causes a substantial change in electrical conductivity of the system.

We study the electronic ground state of pure and ZnO-doped graphene quantum dots using density functional theory calculations. The location of ZnO dimers has been shown to affect the electronic structure of GQD. It has been established that the antiaromatic structure of the meta ZnO dimers contributes to their low bandgap. Contrarily, the bandgap of ortho and para ZnO dimers is modest, with the bandgap of the para ZnO dimer structure being less than that of the ortho ZnO structure. Additionally, it has been noted that the presence of ZnO dimers affects the global chemical indices of GQD. The HOMO-LUMO gap is significantly altered as a result of the systems' HOMO, Fermi level, and LUMO being all pushed to higher energies. This alteration is crucial in many applications since even a minor bandgap shift can alter electrical conductivity. Finally, we can change electronic characteristics of GQDs by adding the ZnO dimers in different sites.
No data were utilized in the analysis presented in the article.
There was no specific funding for this work from any governmental, commercial, or non-profit organization.

All authors participated in the idea and design of this study. Fouad N. Ajeel achieved supervision, computations, and data curation. Salam K. Khamees and Mohammed N. Mutier verified the formal analysis of this work. Kareem H. Mohsin and Hussein Ghafel AL-Toki wrote the original draft of the paper. All authors discussed the results and contributed to writing, review, and editing the final paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors declare no conflict of interest.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Can inorganic salts tune electronic properties of graphene quantum dots?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Colherinhas, E.E. Fileti, and V.V. Chaban, Can inorganic salts tune electronic properties of graphene quantum dots?, Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys., vol. 17, no. 26, pp. 17413–17420, 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of Mo-doped strontium cobaltite on graphene nanosheets for creating a superior electrode in supercapacitor applications
ECS J. Solid State Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.R. Kharangarh and G. Singh, Effect of Mo-doped strontium cobaltite on graphene nanosheets for creating a superior electrode in supercapacitor applications, ECS J. Solid State Sci. Technol., vol. 12, no. 3, p. 031006, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transition metal embedded two-dimensional C3N4–graphene nanocomposite: a multifunctional material

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Ghosh, G. Periyasamy, and S.K. Pati, Transition metal embedded two-dimensional C3N4–graphene nanocomposite: a multifunctional material, J. Phys. Chem. C, vol. 118, no. 28, pp. 15487–15494, 2014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Covalently-controlled properties by design in group IV graphane analogues

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Jiang, M.Q. Arguilla, N.D. Cultrara, and J.E. Goldberger, Covalently-controlled properties by design in group IV graphane analogues, Acc. Chem. Res., vol. 48, no. 1, pp. 144–151, 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chirality-controlled carbon nanotubes fabricated by self-assembly of graphene nanoribbons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Zhang, Z. Peng, and S. Chen, Chirality-controlled carbon nanotubes fabricated by self-assembly of graphene nanoribbons, J. Phys. Chem. C, vol. 118, no. 33, pp. 19477–19483, 2014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical reactivity of electron-doped and hole-doped graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.A. Denis, Chemical reactivity of electron-doped and hole-doped graphene, J. Phys. Chem. C, vol. 117, no. 8, pp. 3895–3902, 2013.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene-related nanomaterials: tuning properties by functionalization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Tang, Z. Zhou, and Z. Chen, Graphene-related nanomaterials: tuning properties by functionalization, Nanoscale, vol. 5, no. 11, pp. 4541–4583, 2013.
